title: Min-Max Construction of Anisotropic Minimal Hypersurfaces
speaker: Guido De Philippis
affiliation: NYU - Courant Institute of Mathematical Science
abstract: We use the min-max construction to find closed hypersurfaces which are stationary with respect to anisotropic elliptic integrands in any closed n-dimensional manifold . These surfaces are regular outside a closed set of zero n-3 dimension. The critical step is to obtain a uniform upper bound for density ratios in the anisotropic min-max construction. This confirms a conjecture posed by Allard. the talk is based on a joint work with A. De Rosa and Y. Li.

proof_stages.label: Even elliptic anisotropic energy setup
proof_stages.summary: The talk starts from classical minimal hypersurfaces as first variations of area and replaces area by a smooth even elliptic integrand depending on position and tangent plane, leading to anisotropic stationarity via a weighted trace of the second fundamental form.

proof_stages.label: Min-max principle and monotonicity obstruction
proof_stages.summary: Classical sweepouts produce saddle-type minimal hypersurfaces for area, but the standard regularity theory relies on the monotonicity formula, which Allard's theorem indicates is unavailable for general anisotropic integrands.

proof_stages.label: Main anisotropic existence theorem and optimality target
proof_stages.summary: After recalling an earlier three-dimensional result allowing one point singularity, the speaker states the new theorem giving nontrivial closed anisotropic minimal hypersurfaces smooth away from a singular set of zero 12-dimensional measure, matching the known optimal regularity scale for minimizers.

proof_stages.label: Generalized min-max varifold scheme
proof_stages.summary: The proof follows the calculus-of-variations program of extracting from sweepouts a stationary generalized hypersurface, modeled as a varifold, and then trying to prove rectifiability, integrality, and smoothness.

proof_stages.label: Isotropic replacement regularization template
proof_stages.summary: For the area functional, Allard rectifiability, Pitts replacements by stable smooth minimizers, and overlapping-annulus replacement/unique-continuation arguments regularize the min-max varifold, but each crucial step uses monotonicity.

proof_stages.label: Anisotropic substitutes for monotonicity
proof_stages.summary: The anisotropic proof substitutes an anisotropic rectifiability theorem plus density from stability, stable curvature estimates, and boundary blow-up analysis in wedges, reducing the remaining obstruction to obtaining local area bounds.

proof_stages.label: Two-dimensional stability area bounds and the old point singularity
proof_stages.summary: In surface dimension two, stability away from the one expected index point yields an 2 curvature estimate and borderline local area bounds away from that point, explaining both the earlier three-dimensional result and why its point singularity could not be removed.

proof_stages.label: Nested cut-and-paste area bounds and multiplicity control
proof_stages.summary: For the general theorem, the min-max construction is refined using nested volume-parameterized sweepouts, triangulation-scale one-sided minimization/cut-and-paste, and prescribed-mean-curvature approximation to force local area bounds and handle multiplicity before sending the curvature parameter to zero.

examples.name: Crystalline surface tension
examples.note: Crystals illustrate why surface energy may depend on tangent direction, while the later discussion emphasizes that genuinely crystalline nonsmooth integrands lie outside the smooth uniformly elliptic theorem.
examples.engagement_reason: Crystalline surface tension is described as motivation with preferred directions and nonsmoothness, but no concrete calculation or construction is carried out.
